content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 28, 1991

Mr. Jim Marino
2340 Miles Street
Bakersfield, CA 93306

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-90-755

Dear Mr. Marino:

This is in response to your questions regarding the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
Since your letter does not cite a specific governmental decision,
we consider it to be a request for informal assistance.2

QUESTIONS

1. As an employee of an engineering company performing
services in connection with a particular project, are you
precluded from participating in your capacity as a planning
commissioner in decisions relating to property within 300 feet of
the project on which your company is working?

2. If your employer is providing services to a project
subject to planning commission review, are you precluded from
participating in decisions relating to that project, if your
employer has no ownership interest in the property?

3. Would the answers to the above questions change in the
event that you are awarded a management position or options to buy
stock in the company that employs you?

_______________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No.  I-90-755
Page 2


     4.  Is your personal participation in a project undertaken
by your employer a factor in determining whether you have a
conflict of interest vis a vis a governmental decision pertaining
to that project?

                         CONCLUSIONS

     1.  You may participate in governmental decisions relating
to a development adjacent to a project for which your employer is
performing services if there would be no reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on your employer resulting from the decisions.

     2.  You may not participate in governmental decisions
relating to a development for which your employer is providing
engineering services unless there would be no financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect public generally, on your employer
resulting from those decisions.

     3.  If you obtain an ownership interest in your employing
company amounting to ten percent or more, then the sources of
income to your employer become sources of income to you, and that
could create a conflict of interest where one of those sources of
income was involved in a planning commission decision.

     4.  Even if you do not personally participate in a project
for which your employer has contracted to provide engineering
services, you may still be required to disqualify yourself from
participating in planning commission decisions relating to that
project.

                         FACTS

     You are a planning commissioner for the City of Bakersfield.
You are also an employee of the DeWalt Corporation ("DeWalt"), a
civil engineering and surveying firm. You are concerned about
participating in governmental decisions regarding projects where
DeWalt is (1) providing engineering services in connection with a
development adjacent to a project under review by the planning
commission and (2) providing services in connection with another
development which is under review by the planning commission.

     You also anticipate that you may be offered a management
position at DeWalt. You would like to know if that anticipated
circumstance would affect your participation in planning
commission decisions. You have inquired in your letter as to
whether ownership of employee stock options would create a
conflict of interest for you in participating in decisions
affecting DeWalt. However, during a subsequent telephone
conversation, you withdrew your question regarding stock options,
and therefore that question will not be addressed in this letter.

Our File No.  I-90-755
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence
any governmental decision in which they know or have reason to
know they have a financial interest.  Whether a public official
has a financial interest in a decision is governed by Section
87103, which provides, in part:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official
or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

* * *

(c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103 (c) and (d).

Planning Commissioners are public officials subject to the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.  (Section 82048.)
Therefore, planning commissioners may not participate in or use
their official position to influence any governmental decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on their economic interests.

Presumably, you receive income of $250 or more per year from
your employer.  Therefore, you must disqualify yourself from any
decision which will foreseeably and materially affect your
employer, where such effect is distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.

Foreseeability

The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur.  To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere

Our File No.  I-90-755
Page 4


possibility; however, certainty is not required.  (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 898-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; In re Pioneer
(1975) 1 FPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed).)  The Act seeks to prevent
more than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even
the appearance of a possible conflict of interest.  (Witt v.
Morrow, supra, at 823.)

     In the circumstances posited by you, it is reasonably
foreseeable that there could be a financial effect upon your
employer where DeWalt is performing services adjacent to a
development which is subject to a planning commission decision.
Many decisions regarding residential housing developments could
have an impact on neighboring developments that are in the
planning stage.  Similarly, where DeWalt itself is providing
services on a project subject to planning commission review, it is
foreseeable that there could be a financial impact on DeWalt
resulting from that review.

     Once it has been determined that it is foreseeable that a
decision could have a financial effect on a public official's
source of income, it must be determined whether the effect will be
material.

Materiality

     The initial step in assessing the materiality of the effect
of a governmental decision requires a determination as to whether
the involvement of the official's economic interest is direct or
indirect.  Regulation 18702.1(b), copy enclosed, states that a
person or business entity is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person or entity, either
personally or by an agent:

     (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

     (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

     (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

Regulation 18702.1(b).

Our File No.  I-90-755
Page 5


     An official's economic interest is also considered to be
directly involved in a decision if there is a nexus between the
purpose for which the official receives income and the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(1))

     There is nexus between the purpose for which an official
receives income and a governmental decision if the official
receives income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.1(d).) If you participate in
planning commission decisions relating to your own engineering
work, you would be using your public position to achieve results
relating to your private work. Under these circumstances, there
would appear to be a nexus between planning commission decisions
and your performance of your private contract. Therefore, you
disqualify yourself from participating in planning commission
decisions relating to the project. (Silver Advice Letter, No. I-
90-644; Lopus Advice Letter, No. I-90-152; copies enclosed.)

     When your economic interest is directly involved in a
decision, you may not participate in the decision unless it will
have no financial effect on that economic interest. (Regulation
18702.1.) Therefore, you may not participate in planning
commission decisions which will have any financial effect upon the
engineering company which is a source of income to you.

     Decisions may also come before the planning commission which
might indirectly affect your company. When a decision will have
an indirect effect on a business entity which has been a source of
income to a public official, the appropriate standard for
determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.2, copy
enclosed. We have not been apprised of the size of DeWalt, and
thus cannot point with particularity to the subdivision of
Regulation 18702.2 that is pertinent to your employer. We suggest
that you review the regulation, utilizing the threshold amounts
that apply to DeWalt, and then determine whether the anticipated
decisions would have effects that would meet the materiality test
that would be applicable to a business entity of that size.

     For example, for a relatively small company, the test for
materiality found in Regulation 18702.2(g) is as follows:

Our File No. I-90-755
Page 6

(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.2(g).

If DeWalt were a company of the appropriate size for
Regulation 18702.2(g), and if a planning commission decision had
an effect that met any of the threshold amounts listed above, then
you could not participate in that decision, because the effect on
DeWalt would be deemed to be material. (See Fitzgerald Advice
Letter, No. I-90-469, copy enclosed.)

Whereas the financial effect of a decision may not always be
easily assessed, it is incumbent upon you as a public official who
has a financial interest to make a good faith effort to determine
to the best of your ability the effect of the decision on your
financial interest, which in this case is your employer. (Weedman
Advice Letter No. I-90-759, copy enclosed.)

The above analysis would not be altered if you obtain a
position of management with DeWalt, unless, in addition to a
management position, you are given ownership amounting to 10
percent or more of the company. Section 82030(a). In that case,
(assuming your ownership interest amounted to at least one
thousand dollars), the sources of income to the company would be
considered to be sources of income to you. (Boyd Advice Letter
No. A-84-281, copy enclosed.) However, if in obtaining a
management position, you remain simply a salaried employee with no
ownership interest, then DeWalt, rather than its clients,
constitutes your source of income. We suggest that you contact us
again if you are given an ownership interest of ten percent of
more in DeWalt, so that we can advise you in connection with your
participation in planning commission decisions.

You have also asked if our analysis would differ with
respect to projects of the developer employing your engineering
firm if you do not personally participate in those projects. As a
general rule, our analysis would be the same. The engineering
firm would still constitute an economic interest, because it would
remain a source of income to you. Thus, any decision which could
have a material financial effect on your firm would require your
disqualification. Additionally, your interests and those of the
firm are linked. This would be even clearer in the event you

Our File No.  I-90-755
Page 7


were promoted to a position of management in the company.
Typically, an employee is paid to promote the employer's
interests, and it would be against the employer's interest to have
any of its projects rejected by the planning commission.  (Silver
Advice Letter, No. I-90-644, copy enclosed.)  Therefore, the above
discussion of nexus issues would be equally applicable here.

I trust the above answers your questions.  If you need
further information, please do not hesitate to call me at (916)
322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        SCOTT HALLABRIN
                                        Acting Chief Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        SUSAN L. BOBROW
                                        Legal Counsel

As an employee of an engineering company performing services in connection with a particular project, are you precluded from participating in your capacity as a planning commissioner in decisions relating to property within 300 feet of the project on which your company is working? 2. If your employer is providing services to a project subject to planning commission review, are you precluded from participating in decisions relating to that project, if your employer has no ownership interest in the property? 3. Is your personal participation in a project undertaken by your employer a factor in determining whether you have a conflict of interest vis a vis a governmental decision pertaining to that project? CONCLUSIONS 1. You may participate in governmental decisions relating to a development adjacent to a project for which your employer is performing services if there would be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on your employer resulting from the decisions. 2. You may not participate in governmental decisions relating to a development for which your employer is providing engineering services unless there would be no financial effect, distinguishable from the effect public generally, on your employer resulting from those decisions. 3.
